key_insight: Physics-informed residual neural networks can model hot deformation and dynamic recrystallization in a Mo-rich titanium alloy more accurately and interpretably than conventional constitutive equations.
engineering_relevance: The approach can improve thermomechanical process design for titanium alloys by predicting flow stress and recrystallization behavior across wide temperature and strain-rate windows.
method: Two stacked residual PINNs were trained on experimental flow-stress curves from 800–1050 °C and 0.01–10 s⁻¹.
An enhanced model enforced constitutive physics such as thermal softening, strain-rate sensitivity, strain hardening, and post-peak softening via automatic differentiation.
A DRX-aware model added dual outputs, JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and Arrhenius consistency to link recrystallization fraction with stress.
evidence_signal: The DRX-aware STAR-PINN reported RMSE 11.69 MPa, MAE 4.83 MPa, R² 0.9850, and cross-validated RMSE 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa while reproducing flow curves, DRX kinetics, and Zener–Hollomon trends.
watch_item: Performance may depend on the quality and coverage of the experimental dataset, and the added physics constraints/latent fusion could require careful tuning to avoid overconstraining the model.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
